topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun cast long, accusing shadows across the worn linoleum floor. I dragged myself to the mailbox, the metal clanging a sound that grated on my nerves. It was just another Tuesday, another day where nothing good seemed to materialize. Then I saw it—a thick, cream-colored card, completely blank save for a single, hastily scribbled 'Happy Birthday.' Unsigned. It lay there, mocking me from the slot, an unsolicited gesture in a world that felt entirely transactional. I picked it up, the paper feeling flimsy and insignificant in my hand. A sharp, involuntary twitch pulled at the corner of my mouth, a fleeting expression of something unpleasant. I crumpled it slowly, the sound of the paper tearing echoing the tightness in my chest. I dropped the mess back into the box, the act feeling like a small, futile rebellion against the emptiness surrounding me.

[story 2]

I stared at the card under the harsh fluorescent light of the kitchen. It was bright, cheerful, utterly devoid of any warmth or context. Another piece of mail, another reminder of connections I wasn't part of. My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath my skin. Why would someone waste ink on something so superficial? I traced the edges of the paper with a fingertip, the texture rough against my skin. A low, simmering resentment began to coil in my gut, a heavy, unwelcome sensation. I tossed it onto the counter, intending to sweep it into the bin immediately, but my hand hesitated. For a moment, I just held it, letting the silence of the apartment amplify the feeling of being overlooked, of being irrelevant. Then, with a decisive shove, it landed in the trash, and I turned away, focusing intensely on the chipped pattern on the cabinet door.

[story 3]

The sound of the mail slot opening felt like an intrusion. I didn't bother looking; I just snatched the envelope, my movements jerky and impatient. Inside, the card was folded neatly. Unsigned. The sheer lack of personalization struck me like a physical blow. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I unfolded it, and the simple message—a birthday wish—felt like a deliberate slight. I felt a surge of irritation, hot and prickly, rising up my neck. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the strange feeling down, to suppress the urge to lash out at the randomness of it all. When I finally opened my eyes, the card was already halfway to the wastebasket, tossed down without ceremony, a silent declaration that I wasn't interested in anything offered freely.
